title: Number Systems
summary: Binary, decimal, and hexadecimal — the languages digital hardware speaks.

sections.heading: Positional Number Systems
sections.body: A number system's base (radix) determines how many digits are available and the weight of each position. Digital logic uses base-2 (binary) natively; humans prefer base-10; engineers use base-16 (hex) as a compact binary shorthand.

sections.heading: Binary Representation
sections.body: Each bit position carries a power-of-two weight. The n-bit unsigned value is the sum of bitᵢ × 2ⁱ. This maps directly to wires in hardware — one wire per bit.
bullets: MSB (most significant bit) has highest weight

sections.heading: Hexadecimal Shorthand
sections.body: One hex digit encodes exactly four binary bits (a nibble). This makes memory addresses, bus values, and register dumps readable without long binary strings.

sections.heading: Signed Numbers (Two's Complement)
sections.body: Signed integers use two's complement: the MSB is the sign bit (0 = positive, 1 = negative). To negate N, invert all bits and add 1. This allows adders to perform subtraction with the same hardware.
bullets: Sign extension replicates MSB when widening

sections.heading: Overflow and Range
sections.body: When an arithmetic result exceeds the representable range of n bits, overflow occurs. Hardware sets overflow flags in ALUs; RTL designers must size buses correctly or detect overflow explicitly.

quiz.question: How many bits does one hex digit represent?
quiz.explanation: Each hex digit maps to exactly 4 binary bits (one nibble).

quiz.question: MSB stands for:
quiz.explanation: MSB is the highest-weight bit in a binary number.

quiz.question: Sign extension of 1011 (4-bit) to 8 bits gives:

flashcards.front: How many values can n bits represent (unsigned)?
